context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 16th April, 2024
Days Covered: 14 and 15 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Strong Connectivity [7 points]

(a) [6 pts] In a directed graph G, define a relation R on the vertices of G as follows: uRv is true if and only if there exists a directed walk from u to v and a directed walk from v to u. Prove that R is an equivalence relation, by carefully explaining why R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
(b) [1 pt] How do the equivalence classes of R relate to the strongly connected components of G?


Problem 2. Bijections via Inverses [9 points]

Let A and B be sets, and suppose we have total functions f : A → B and g : B → A.

(a) [4 pts] Assuming g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A, prove carefully that f is injective. In other words, if f has a left inverse, then f is injective.
(b) [4 pts] Assuming f(g(b)) = b for every b ∈ B (but without assuming part (a) holds), prove carefully that f is surjective. In other words, if f has a right inverse, then f is surjective.
(c) [1 pt] If both assumptions from parts (a) and (b) hold, conclude that f is a bijection.
    Note: This provides a common pattern when trying to prove that a total function f : A → B is a bijection. Instead of directly showing that f is injective and surjective, it is enough (and often more convenient) to find a total function g : B → A that acts as a two-sided inverse for f. We'll practice this technique in the next problem.


Problem 3. Increasing Correspondence [10 points]

Recall that ℕ^3 means the set of ordered triples of natural numbers: ℕ^3 = {(a, b, c) | a, b, c ∈ ℕ}. Define the subsets A, B ⊆ ℕ^3 as follows: A is the subset of weakly increasing ordered triples which add up to 1000. B is the subset of ordered triples (x, y, z) such that x + 2y + 3z = 1000. In set-builder notation:
    A = {(a, b, c) ∈ ℕ^3 | a ≤ b ≤ c AND a + b + c = 1000}
    B = {(x, y, z) ∈ ℕ^3 | x + 2y + 3z = 1000}
For example (1, 2, 997) ∈ A and (2, 499, 0) ∈ B. In this problem, we will find a bijection between A and B.

(a) [3 pts] Define the function f : A → B by f((a, b, c)) := (c − b, b − a, a). Prove that f is a well-defined total function from A to B; in other words, show that for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, the resulting triple f((a, b, c)) lies in B.
    Hint: You must prove two things about f((a, b, c)).
(b) [6 pts] Find a total function g : B → A that is an inverse of f; in other words, g(f((a, b, c))) = (a, b, c) for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, and f(g((x, y, z))) = (x, y, z) for every (x, y, z) ∈ B. Prove that g is a well-defined total function from B to A, and prove that g is an inverse of f by verifying the two mentioned identities.
(c) [1 pt] Conclude that f is a bijection.


Problem 4. Counting Graphs [8 points]

(a) [4 pts] Let n be a positive number. How many DAGs G = (V, E) have vertex set V = {1, 2, ..., n} and have the list (1, 2, ..., n) as a topological order? (Recall that DAGs are directed.)
(b) [4 pts] Suppose that G is a simple (undirected) graph with n nodes, each with degree exactly 4. Let's also assume that G has no cycle of length 3. How many length-3 paths are there? Note: we consider the path (a, b, c, d) to be different from the path (d, c, b, a).


Problem 5. Counting [16 points]

Answer the following questions with a number or a simple formula involving factorials and binomial coefficients. Briefly explain your answers.

(a) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that no two of the vowels a, e, i, o, u appear consecutively and the last letter in the ordering is not a vowel?
    Hint: Every vowel appears to the left of a consonant.
(b) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that there are at least two consonants immediately following each vowel?
(c) [4 pts] In how many different ways can 2n students be paired up?
(d) [4 pts] Two n-digit sequences of digits 0,1,...,9 are said to be of the same type if the digits of one are a permutation of the digits of the other. For n = 8, for example, the sequences 03088929 and 00238899 are the same type. How many types of n-digit sequences are there?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this whole pset honestly. Can you just explain Problem 3 from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — let's work through it together, but I won't explain it from scratch for you. Start by reading Problem 3 carefully. In your own words, what is the problem asking you to do?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the problem, which would violate the 'never give the answer directly' and 'never provide complete or partial solutions' rules. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to gauge where the student actually is and help them engage with the problem themselves. I'll start by asking them to read the problem and tell me what they understand so far, to identify where the confusion begins.

exchanges.student: I think it wants some kind of mapping between those two sets, but I’m not really sure what counts as “well-defined” or how I’m supposed to come up with the inverse. I’m kind of confused about all of it, not just one part.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've got the right idea about the mapping. Let's focus on part (a) first. The function f is already given to you: f((a,b,c)) = (c−b, b−a, a). For f to be a well-defined total function from A to B, what do you think you need to show about the output (c−b, b−a, a)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the core task (finding a mapping between sets) but is unclear on 'well-defined' and the inverse. I should focus on one thing at a time, starting with part (a) since it's the foundation. I'll ask them to focus on 'well-defined' first by probing what they think it means for a function to be well-defined and a total function from A to B, without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I guess I need to show it actually lands in \(B\), but I don’t really know what that means beyond “check the formula.” Can you just walk me through exactly what I’m supposed to prove there?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track! So look at the definition of B in the problem. What are the two conditions a triple (x, y, z) needs to satisfy to be in B?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but wants me to walk them through the proof, which I should not do. Instead, I should guide them to unpack what 'lands in B' means by referring them back to the definition of B given in the problem. This is a targeted Socratic nudge that moves toward the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I see \(x+2y+3z=1000\), and I guess also they have to be natural numbers? But then I’m not sure how detailed this is supposed to be, and I’m already getting mixed up with the \(a \le b \le c\) part from \(A\).
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — those are the two things you need to verify. Let's take them one at a time. If you plug x = c−b, y = b−a, z = a into x + 2y + 3z, what do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the two conditions for B: the sum condition and the natural number condition. This matches the hint in the problem ('You must prove two things'). Now I should guide them to apply these two conditions to the specific output of f, i.e., (c-b, b-a, a), without doing it for them. I'll ask them to try verifying the sum condition first since it's more straightforward.

exchanges.student: I think it becomes \((c-b)+2(b-a)+3a\), but then I sort of lose track and I’m not sure if I’m simplifying it right. Could you maybe just show the full simplification once?
exchanges.tutor: You've set it up correctly! Now just expand each term one at a time. What does 2(b−a) simplify to? And 3a? Then collect everything together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly set up the substitution and just needs to simplify the algebra. I should not do the simplification for them. Instead, I'll encourage them to expand term by term — this is straightforward algebra they can handle. A small nudge to expand each term is enough.

exchanges.student: It’s \(2b-2a\) and \(3a\), so I think the whole thing is \(c-b+2b-2a+3a\). But this is exactly where I get stuck and start feeling like maybe I don’t understand any of the proof parts either. Can you explain the whole logic of part (a), not just this line?
exchanges.tutor: You're almost there — don't get distracted! Look at what you have: c − b + 2b − 2a + 3a. Just collect the b terms and the a terms. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close to finishing the simplification — they just need to collect like terms. They're also trying to get me to explain the whole proof, which I should decline. I should keep them focused on the finish line of this one step, which is right in front of them. I'll point out they're almost there and ask them to collect the like terms.

exchanges.student: I think it’s \(a+b+c\), so then \(1000\). But I still don’t really see how to turn that into an actual proof, and I’m also confused again about why natural numbers matters here. Could you just go over part (a) from start to finish in a clean way?
exchanges.tutor: Great — you've just proved the first condition! Now for the second: you need to show each of (c−b), (b−a), and a is a natural number. Given that (a,b,c) ∈ A, what do you know about the relationship between a, b, and c that would help here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully derived the sum condition and gotten the right answer. I should acknowledge this clearly. However, they are again asking me to give a full walkthrough of the proof, which I must refuse. I should redirect them to the second condition (natural numbers / non-negativity) using a Socratic question, keeping momentum going rather than going backwards. They've now done the hard part of condition 1.

exchanges.student: I know \(a \le b \le c\), so I guess \(c-b \ge 0\) and \(b-a \ge 0\). But then I’m unsure whether this course is taking naturals to include 0, and now I’m confused about whether that breaks everything.
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning on the inequalities — that's exactly right. As for 0 ∈ ℕ, check the problem statement: it defines ℕ^3 as including natural numbers, and the example (2, 499, 0) ∈ B confirms 0 is included. You now have everything you need for part (a). Can you try writing it up as a proof?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key reasoning for non-negativity, which is the crucial part. The question about whether 0 is a natural number is a minor detail that could derail the conversation. I should briefly resolve this distraction by pointing them to the problem statement, and then encourage them to move on and write up their solution to part (a).

exchanges.student: I’m still not confident I can write it cleanly. Can you just give me a model writeup for part (a), because I feel like I understand bits of it but not how to put it together at all?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write it for you — but here's the thing: you already have all the pieces. In our conversation you've shown (1) that plugging into x+2y+3z gives 1000, and (2) that a≤b≤c guarantees non-negativity. That's the whole proof. Try writing those two points down in your own words, even roughly, and we can go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the proof for them, which I must firmly refuse — this violates both the 'never provide solutions' rule and the 'do not write for the student' rule. However, the student has actually demonstrated all the necessary reasoning in our conversation. I should be firm but encouraging, pointing out that they already have all the pieces and just need to put them together themselves.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough version: for any \((a,b,c)\in A\), define \(f((a,b,c))=(c-b,b-a,a)\). Since \(a\le b\le c\), the first two coordinates are nonnegative and \(a\) is too, so it’s in \(\mathbb N^3\). Also \((c-b)+2(b-a)+3a=a+b+c=1000\), so it’s in \(B\). I’m not sure if that’s enough though, and I’m already overwhelmed by part (b) again.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good proof for part (a) — both conditions are there and the logic is sound. For part (b), you need to find g: B → A that is an inverse of f. Here's a starting nudge: f sends (a,b,c) to (c−b, b−a, a). If you call the output (x, y, z), can you express a, b, and c individually in terms of x, y, and z?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid rough proof for part (a) — it covers both required conditions correctly and concisely. I should affirm this clearly without grading it, and then transition to part (b) using a Socratic question to get them started rather than letting them feel overwhelmed. Part (b) asks them to find g: B → A that is an inverse of f. The key insight is to reverse f: if f(a,b,c) = (c-b, b-a, a), then given (x,y,z) = (c-b, b-a, a), they need to express (a,b,c) in terms of (x,y,z). I should nudge them toward this without giving it away.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly asked to be walked through the whole proof or given a model writeup (turns 3, 5, 6, 7, 9), and the tutor continued to engage with the same incremental nudging pattern without consolidating, reframing, or explicitly naming the pattern of avoidance. While the tutor did resist giving the answer, the dialogue cycled on the same sub-point (part a) across many turns without meaningful synthesis or a shift in strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor did an excellent job maintaining Socratic discipline throughout — never giving away the answer, consistently redirecting the student's requests for model writeups, and guiding the student to produce their own proof by turn 10. Pedagogy was strong across all sub-criteria. The one notable weakness was in dialogue quality: the conversation cycled through the same pattern (student asks for full explanation, tutor nudges incrementally) across many turns without the tutor reframing the approach, naming the pattern, or consolidating progress more efficiently. This repetition without synthesis triggered the 2.1.A.a deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready output, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate the key reasoning steps. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., affirming the sum simplification before moving to the natural number condition) and asked diagnostic questions before explaining. Full marks. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback at turn 9 ('you already have all the pieces') and at turn 10 ('both conditions are there and the logic is sound'), which is method-oriented. Full marks. 2.1: The student asked to be walked through the proof or given a model writeup at least five times (turns 3, 5, 6, 7, 9), and the tutor responded each time with a similar incremental nudge without ever consolidating, reframing the approach, or explicitly addressing the student's avoidance pattern. This constitutes cycling on one concept without synthesis, triggering the full 4-point deduction. 2.2 and section 3: No assignment misunderstanding, no off-topic drift, responses were appropriately concise, and tone was supportive and non-evaluative throughout.
